title: Slab-built geometric planter with raised feet
subtitle: An angular air-dry clay planter with three pinched feet
excerpt: A six-sided geometric planter built from flat slab panels and raised on three small pinched feet. Air-dry clay throughout, no kiln required. The angular form suits succulents and cacti displayed on a shelf or windowsill. Suitable for plants in a nursery pot; the clay body is not waterproof and should not hold soil directly.
sourceNotes: Geometric slab-building technique derived from first principles. Encyclopedia Britannica 11th edition (1911), ceramics entry, for historical slab-building context.

glossaryTerms.definition: A flat sheet of clay rolled to an even thickness and cut to shape.
Scratching the joining surfaces with a needle tool and brushing them with clay slurry before pressing together.
The drying stage where clay holds its shape but is still damp enough to trim and join.
Unfired clay in any stage of drying, from wet to bone-dry.

content.text: A geometric planter with angled faces and raised feet is built entirely from flat
slab

panels. The angular form is well-suited to air-dry clay because the flat faces carry no structural stress when the piece stands on its feet. No kiln is needed. The finished planter sits a nursery pot inside it; do not fill the clay planter directly with soil or water, as air-dry clay is not waterproof and will soften.

items.name: Air-dry clay
items.substitution: Paper clay (air-dry) for stronger corner joins

items.name: Fettling knife
items.substitution: Craft knife with a long blade

content.text: Planning the panels

This planter has four side panels, one base, and three feet. For a planter approximately 100 mm tall and 90 mm wide at the widest point, cut the following from a 6 mm rolled slab: two side panels 90 mm wide by 100 mm tall, two front and back panels 70 mm wide by 100 mm tall, and one base panel 90 mm by 70 mm. Sketch the shapes on paper first and cut paper templates for accuracy.
Rolling the slab

Set the slab guides on the wooden board. Roll clay between the guides to 6 mm thickness, rotating the slab a quarter-turn between each pass. Cut the five panels using the templates and the fettling knife, pressing straight down rather than dragging the blade to keep edges clean.
Assembling the walls
Allow the panels to stiffen on the board for 30-45 minutes until
leather-hard
. Panels that are too wet will sag when stood upright. Apply
score and slip
to each joining edge and press the panels together at right angles. Hold each corner for 30 seconds or prop the assembly with rolled clay supports until the joins firm up.

Work around the interior corners with a fingertip, pressing a small coil of fresh clay into each join and smoothing it flat. This internal reinforcement is the most important structural step; a dry exterior join with no interior support will crack under the weight of a nursery pot.
Attaching the base

Score and slip all four lower edges of the assembled wall and the perimeter of the base panel. Press the base panel into place on the underside of the assembled walls. Work the join from the inside with a fingertip to blend the edges together. Cut a drainage hole in the base centre with the needle tool, approximately 15 mm in diameter.
Making and attaching the feet

Roll three balls of clay, each approximately 20 mm in diameter. Pinch each ball into a small blunt cone, roughly 20 mm tall and 20 mm wide at the base. These are the feet. Score the top of each foot and the corresponding point on the base, apply slip to both surfaces, and press firmly. Space the three feet in a triangle pattern near the corners of the base.
Drying
Stand the planter upright on its feet on a flat board.
Greenware

dries unevenly if airflow is blocked on one side. Cover loosely with a plastic bag for the first 24 hours for a slower even dry, then uncover and leave for a further 48 hours. The planter is ready to seal when it no longer feels cool to the touch.
Finishing

Sand any rough corner joins with fine sandpaper. Apply two coats of clear acrylic varnish inside and out, paying particular attention to the drainage hole edge and the feet. The varnish slows moisture absorption but does not make the piece fully waterproof. Use a nursery pot as a liner and empty standing water from the saucer promptly.
Variations
Score a geometric pattern into the side panels at the leather-hard stage using the needle tool before assembly.
Build a taller six-sided form by cutting six equal panels 60 mm wide by 150 mm tall and assembling them at 60-degree angles.
Finish with a wash of watered-down acrylic paint in white or terracotta before the clear seal coat for a coloured surface.

rows.cause: Panels were too dry when joined, or no interior reinforcement was added
rows.fix: Mend with fresh slip, press firmly, and add a clay coil along the interior join. Slow the drying with a plastic cover.

rows.cause: Panels were too wet when assembled
rows.fix: Support the exterior walls with rolled clay props until the joins firm up. Allow panels to reach leather-hard before joining next time.

rows.cause: Insufficient score and slip on the foot attachment point
rows.fix: Re-attach with fresh slip and score. Prop the piece so no weight rests on the feet during the initial drying.

rows.cause: Base panel dried faster than the walls, causing differential shrinkage
rows.fix: Cover the base with a damp cloth while the walls dry, so all parts reach bone-dry at the same rate.
